Wit、unker、Git：失落的中世纪英语亲密代词

Wit, unker, Git: The lost medieval pronouns of English intimacy

www.bbc.com 作者 eigenspace 分数 198 评论 126 2026/04/09 18:10
打开 HN 原帖 打开原始文章

中文摘要

  1. 21 小时前Sophie HardachGetty Images 1000 年前的爱情和冒险故事揭示了一系列现已灭绝的英语代词，令人眼花缭乱。它们捕捉到了人们曾经如何看待“二元性”的独特之处。但他们为什么会灭绝呢？你会用哪个词来形容你自己？ “我”大概是单数。那么你和一群人呢？ “我们”当然是复数形式。但是你和另外一个人呢？现代英语中没有这个词。你可能只会使用“我们”或“我们两个”。但在一千多年前，你可能会说：“机智”。这个词曾经也被亲切地用来形容两个人之间的亲密关系，但它是在几个世纪以来巨大的社会和政治变革中丢失或转变的许多人称代词之一。英语已经变得简单——但有时这会留下空白，造成混乱。“Wit”在古英语中的意思是“我们两个”，这是一种直到大约 12 世纪才在英格兰使用的日耳曼语言，后来演变成了我们今天所说的英语。现在完全失去了，“wit”是一组已灭绝的代词的一部分，用于恰好两个人：双重形式，其中还包括“uncer”或“unker”（“我们的”两个人）和“git”（“你们两个”）。这种双重形式在 13 世纪左右从英语中消失了。 （你可以在本文后面的短片中听到其中一些是如何发音的。）爱尔兰科克大学古英语和古挪威语教授汤姆·伯克特（Tom Birkett）说，“[人称]代词有完整的历史”，包括维京人和诺曼人入侵对英语的影响，以及改变我们说话方式的规范和习俗的变化。伯克特说，许多古英语代词仍在使用。伯克特说，我们最古老的英语人称代词包括“他”和“它”，以及“我们”、“我们”、“我们的”、“我”和“我的”。它们经历了 1000 多年的历史和剧变，几乎完好无损。“‘He’绝对是一种非常古老的英语形式，还有‘hit’，它失去了‘h’，变成了‘it’，”伯克特说。古英语的“Ic”也一直保持着弹性，只丢失了一个字母，就变成了现代英语的“I”。但其他代词被抛弃了——比如曾经常见的双元形式。 Birkett 说：“这种说法在古英语文本中相当普遍。特别是在诗歌中，我们使用‘wit’和‘unc’来表示‘我们两个，我们两个’。”为了说明二元的诗意力量，Birkett 举了一首爱情诗的例子，这首诗被称为 Wulf 和 Eadwacer，已有 1000 多年的历史。在这首诗中，一个女人渴望她的爱人沃尔夫，因为他被她的家族拒绝而与她分离。最后一行用现代英语翻译是：“一个人可以轻易地分裂从未联合过的东西，我们两个人的歌。”在古英语原文中，“我们两个人的歌”的词是“uncer giedd”——意思是“我们的歌”，但只适合两个人。“那首诗中使用了双重代词，我认为这是一个非常亲密的用法，因为它都是关于‘我们两个一起对抗世界’，”伯克特说。 “当然在诗歌中，它具有在两个人之间建立亲密联系的用途。” 与“hronfixas”战斗 在《贝奥武夫》中，二元形象戏剧性地出现：两个战士手持剑在海中游泳，“以保护我们两个免受鲸鱼的侵害”（原作中的“wit unc wið hronfixas werian”）。 《贝奥武夫》被认为写于 8 世纪，是最早用白话（普遍使用的语言）而非高雅文化或文学语言写成的欧洲史诗。1066 年诺曼征服后，从古英语到中古英语的过渡中，这种双重形式幸存下来，但随后消失了。 “这是一整类刚刚消失的代词，”伯克特说。根据他的说法，双重出现的最后一次是在“丹麦人哈维洛克”中，这是一位大约 1300 年的不知名作者的文字：“罗伯特！威廉！Hware ar ye？Gripeth eþer unker a god tre，晚了我们没有 þise doges fle。”（“罗伯特！威廉！你们都在哪里？你们两个都拿了一根好棍子，我们不要让这只狗逃离！”）介绍“她”的双重形式在某些语言中仍然存在，例如阿拉伯语。但为什么这样一个诗意的代词在英语中消失了呢？当流行文化今天仍然颂扬这种特殊的二元性感觉并且“只有我们两个”的流行遍及歌曲和文学时，这似乎尤其奇怪。伯克特说，一般来说，“语言趋于简单”。在他看来，鉴于广义的复数“我们”也可以用于两个人，因此可能根本没有足够充分的理由做出额外的努力来保持双重形式的活力。他指出，事实上，许多其他古英语代词也没有幸存到现代，而是被外语单词或更有用的替代词所取代。例如，“她”比“他”年轻，似乎是两个古英语女性代词的合并，Birkett 说 – “heo”和“seo”。他说：“[这些]可能随着时间的推移结合在一起，形成了‘她’。”维京人和狼人另一个常用的现代代词“they”——以及“them”和“their”——实际上根本不是古英语，据伯克特说。它与古挪威语一起到来，这是一种斯堪的纳维亚语言，由从 800 年代起入侵并定居英格兰的维京人使用。然后，“they”传播并取代了古英语的“hie”。伯克特认为，外来的“they”可能因实际原因而变得流行：本土的“hie”可能会令人困惑，因为它可能意味着“they”，但也可能意味着“her”——而“they”是独特的，因此更清晰。后来，“they”也偶尔以单数形式使用，就像今天用作中性代词一样，伯克特说。例如，单数“they”出现在 14 世纪的文本《威廉与狼人》中，以及大约在同一时期由杰弗里·乔叟撰写的《赎罪券商序言》中。“乔叟在 14 世纪就使用‘they’作为单数，”伯克特说。 “这是一个非常非常古老的用法，当你不认识这个人，[并且不想称呼]他们为‘他’或‘她’时，它非常有用。” 盖蒂图片社 双重性经常被描绘成特殊的，就像在欧洲 12 世纪中叶的场景中一样（图片来源：Getty Images）你们，你们所有人，你们。在 1066 年诺曼人征服英格兰后的几个世纪里，我们的语言经历了另一场深刻的变化：我们开始使用“你们”来表达对一个人，也对许多人。以前对此有不同的说法。在古英语中，“Þu”（后来拼写为“thou”）是“你（单数）”的意思。另一个词“ge”在一些英语地区保留为“ye”，用于表示“you（复数）”。随着诺曼征服，英国开启了变革性多语言的又一章，特别是英语和法语之间的密切接触。诺曼法语中的“vous”传入英国，既可以用来称呼一群人，也可以在正式场合中只称呼一个人。伯克特说，当只称呼一个人时，母语英语的复数形式也被用作单数形式的尊重标志。“很自然地将[法语‘vous’]扩展到英语，并使用复数‘you’形式来与国王和贵族交谈，”他说。 “然后它被用作对高级职位的人的尊重，然后，最终，对每个人都使用。”在这个过程中，“你”、“你”和“你的”消失了，取而代之的是包罗万象的“你”。“所以你看到了诺曼法语和贵族的政治，以及法语对英语的影响，当然这种影响是广泛的，”伯克特说。他说，如今，爱尔兰和其他地方的某些英语方言仍然区分复数和单数的“you”。 “在我所在的爱尔兰明斯特地区，‘ye’非常非常常用作复数形式。人们不会倾向于把它写下来，但在英语口语中它被大量使用，”他说。在格拉斯哥和苏格兰中西部，另一个版本“youse”在当地方言中经常被用作复数。而今天的人们也在日常生活中自发地使用变通办法来澄清复数，例如“you all”和“you girls”。伯克特说，尽管有这些变化，与名词和动词相比，人称代词仍然相当稳定，并保留了一些从英语名词中完全消失的古英语语法特征。例如，我们仍然根据情况说“he”、“his”或“him”，而英语名词和形容词过去也根据情况变化，现在不再这样做。例如，在古英语中，“king”这个词——“cyning”——根据它在句子中的角色而变化：“Hē is cyning”是主格，意思是“他是国王”，而“mid Þæm cyninge”是在主格中。与格，意思是“与国王”。 “[人称代词]往往能够生存下来，因为它们是语言的基石，”伯克特说。 “它们每天、每时每刻都在使用，而且它们的变化肯定比[英语]语言中的名词或动词要少。代词具有这种持久力。”已经绝迹的英语双代词是否有可能有一天再次回归，将比尔·威瑟斯的《Just the Two of Us》变成《Just Wit》，将泰勒·斯威夫特的《Our Song》变成《Uncer Song》？根据伯克特的历史例子，卷土重来似乎不太可能：一旦双元不再使用，它​​就不会再出现。然而，可以肯定的是，代词的未来是我们想要的。也许机智的人——你和我——可以开始，在我们今天的对话中加入一些失落的代词？——如果你喜欢这个故事，请订阅《基本列表》时事通讯——精心挑选的专题、视频和不容错过的新闻，每周两次发送到你的收件箱。有关 BBC 的更多科学、技术、环境和健康故事，请在 Facebook 和 Instagram 上关注我们。

Original Summary

  1. 21 hours agoSophie HardachGetty ImagesTales of love and adventure from 1,000 years ago reveal a dazzling range of now-extinct English pronouns. They capture something unique about how people once thought about "two-ness". But why did they die out in the first place?Which word would you use to refer to yourself? "I", presumably, in the singular. And how about you and a group of people? "We", of course, in the plural.But how about you and one other person? In modern English, there is no word for that. You would probably just use "we" or "the two of us".But more than 1,000 years ago, you would have said: "wit".This term, once also used affectionately to describe the closeness between two people, is one of many personal pronouns that have been lost or transformed amid huge social and political change over the centuries. The English language has become simplified – but at times this has left gaps, creating confusion."Wit" means "we two" in Old English, a Germanic language spoken in England until about the 12th Century, which evolved into the English we speak today. Now completely lost, "wit" was part of an extinct group of pronouns used for exactly two people: the dual form, which also includes "uncer" or "unker" ("our" for two people) and "git" ("you two"). That dual form vanished from the English language around the 13th Century. (You can hear how some of these were pronounced in the short clips later in this article.)"There's a whole history in the [personal] pronouns", including the impact of Viking and Norman invasions on the English language alongside shifting norms and customs that have changed how we talk, says Tom Birkett, a professor of Old English and Old Norse at University College Cork in Ireland.Many Old English pronouns are still in use, says Birkett. Our oldest English personal pronouns include "he" and "it", as well as "we", "us", "our", "me" and "mine", Birkett says. They have made it through more than 1,000 years of history and upheaval, almost intact."'He' definitely is a very old English form, and also 'hit', which lost the 'h' and became 'it'," Birkett says. The Old English "Ic" has also been resilient, losing only one letter, to become the modern English "I".But other pronouns were cast off – such as the once-common dual form. "It's fairly widespread in Old English texts. Particularly in poetry, we get the use of 'wit' and 'unc' for 'us two, the two of us'," says Birkett.To illustrate the poetic power of the dual, Birkett gives the example of a love poem, known as Wulf and Eadwacer, that is over 1,000 years old. In the poem, a woman yearns for her lover, Wulf, who is separated from her because he was rejected by her clan. The last line reads, in a modern English translation:"One can easily split what was never united,the song of the two of us."In the Old English original, the words for "the song of the two of us" are "uncer giedd" – meaning "our song", but just for two people."The dual pronoun is used in that poem, and I think it's quite an intimate use, because it's all about 'We two together against the world'," says Birkett. "Certainly in poetry, it has that use of creating an intimate connection between two people."Fighting the "hronfixas"In the Beowulf, the dual makes a dramatic appearance: two warriors swim in the sea holding swords, "to defend the two of us against whales" ("wit unc wið hronfixas werian" in the original). Thought to be written in the 8th Century, Beowulf is the earliest European epic written in the vernacular – the language commonly spoken – rather than a high culture, or literary language.The dual form survived the transition from Old English to Middle English, after the Norman conquest in 1066, but then disappeared. "That's a whole category of pronouns that's just been lost," says Birkett. According to him, one of the last times the dual appears is in "Havelok the Dane", a text by an unknown author from around 1300:"Roberd! Willam! Hware ar ye? Gripeth eþer unker a god tre, and late we nouth þise doges fle."("Robert! William! Where are [you all]? Both of you two grab a good staff, and let's not allow this dog to flee!")Introducing "she"The dual form still exists in some languages, such as Arabic. But why did such a poetic pronoun go extinct in English? It seems especially strange when popular culture still celebrates that sense of a special two-ness today and the prevalence of "just the two of us" pervades song and literature.Generally speaking, "language tends towards simplicity", Birkett says. Given that the broad, plural "we" can also be used for two people, there may simply not have been a strong enough reason to make the extra effort of keeping the dual form alive, in his view. He points out that many other Old English pronouns have, in fact, not survived to the modern day either – replaced by words from foreign languages or more useful alternatives."She", for example, is younger than "he", and seems to be an amalgamation of two Old English female pronouns, Birkett says – "heo" and "seo". "[These] probably combined over time, to make 'she'," he says.Vikings and werewolvesAnother commonly used modern pronoun, "they" – along with "them" and "their" – is actually not Old English at all, according to Birkett. It arrived with Old Norse, a Scandinavian language spoken by the Vikings who invaded and settled in England from the 800s onwards. "They" then spread and replaced the Old English "hie".The foreign "they" may have become popular for practical reasons, Birkett suggests: the native "hie" was potentially confusing as it could mean "they" but also, "her" – whereas "they" was distinct and therefore clearer.Later, "they" was also occasionally used in the singular, as it is today when used as a gender-neutral pronoun, Birkett says. The singular "they" appears, for example, in the 14th-Century text "William and the Werewolf", as well as in "The Pardoner's Prologue", by Geoffrey Chaucer, written around the same time."Chaucer was using 'they' as a singular back in the 14h Century," says Birkett. "It's a very, very old usage, and very useful when you don't know the person, [and don't want to refer] to them as 'he' or 'she'."Getty ImagesTwo-ness is often portrayed as special, as in this European mid-12th Century scene (Credit: Getty Images)You, you all, you guysIn the centuries after the Norman conquest of England in 1066, our language underwent another profound change: we began using "you" to address one person, and also, many people.Before, there had been different words for that. In Old English, "Þu" (later spelled "thou") was the word for "you (singular)". A different word, "ge", which has survived in some English-speaking regions as "ye", was used for "you (plural)". With the Norman conquest, another chapter of transformative multilingualism began in England, in particular, intense contact between English and French. The Norman French "vous" arrived in England, which is used to address both a group, and also, in a formal context, only one person. The native English plural was then also used as a mark of respect in the singular, when addressing just one person, Birkett says."It was natural to extend that [French 'vous'] to English and to use that plural 'you' form to talk to the king and to the aristocracy," he says. "And then it was used as a respectful term for people in senior positions, and then, eventually, for everybody."In the process, "thou", "thee" and "thine" disappeared, replaced by the catch-all "you"."So you've got the politics there, of Norman French and the aristocracy, and the influence of French on English, which of course has been extensive," Birkett says. Today, some dialects of English spoken in Ireland and elsewhere still make distinction between the plural and singular you, he says. "In my area, in Munster in Ireland, 'ye' is very, very commonly used as a plural. People wouldn't tend to write it down as much, but in spoken English it's used a lot," he says. In Glasgow and west central Scotland, another version, "youse", is often used as the plural in the local dialect.And people today also use spontaneous workarounds to clarify the plural in everyday life, such as "you all" and "you guys".Despite these changes, Birkett says that compared to nouns and verbs, personal pronouns have remained quite stable and retain some grammatical features of Old English that have entirely disappeared from English nouns. For example, we still say "he", "his" or "him", depending on the case – whereas English nouns and adjectives, which in the past also changed depending on the case, no longer do.In Old English, for instance, the word for "king" – "cyning" – changed depending on its role in a sentence: "Hē is cyning" is in the nominative case and means "he is king", whereas "mid Þæm cyninge" is in the dative case and means "with the king". "[Personal pronouns] have tended to survive because they're the bedrock of language," says Birkett. "They're used every day, all the time, and they've certainly changed less than nouns or verbs in the [English] language. Pronouns have had that kind of staying power." Is there any chance the extinct English dual pronouns might return one day, turning Bill Withers' "Just the Two of Us" into "Just Wit", and Taylor Swift's "Our Song" into "Uncer Song"? Based on Birkett's historical examples, a comeback seems unlikely: once the dual fell out of use, it did not reappear. However, surely, the future of our pronouns is whatever we want it to be. Perhaps wit – you and me – could make a start, and sprinkle some lost pronouns into our conversations today?--If you liked this story, sign up for The Essential List newsletter – a handpicked selection of features, videos and can't-miss news, delivered to your inbox twice a week. For more science, technology, environment and health stories from the BBC, follow us on Facebook and Instagram.

评论（默认中文）

red_admiral 2026/04/09 22:03
英语曾经有双重代词（文章是关于什么的）、正确的宾格和属格（她/她/她、谁/谁和撇号的属格是幸存者）、正式/非正式的第二人称代词（你/你）以及当你学习法语或拉丁语时会出现的许多其他东西。

是/否和是/否也曾经表示不同的意思：“这是正确的吗？”可以回答“是的，这是正确的”，而“这不是一个错误吗？”可以回答“是的，这是正确的”（您也可以通过字面上的“不”来解析）。

“军事法庭”和“秘书长”就是保留原始名词第一词序的例子。

English used to have dual pronouns (what the article is a about), proper accusatives and genitives (she/her/hers, who/whom and the apostrophe-s genitive are survivors), formal/informal 2nd person pronouns (you / thou) and quite a few other things that come up when you learn French or Latin.

Yes/No and Yea/Nay used to mean different things too: "Is this correct?" could be answered "Yea, it is correct" whereas "Is this not a mistake?" could be answered "Yes, it is correct" (which you can also parse by taking the 'not' literally).

"Courts martial" and "secretaries general" are examples where the original noun-first word order remains.

在 HN 中查看
pdpi 2026/04/10 00:51
作为一个讲葡萄牙语的人，正式/非正式的第二人称对我来说很着迷。

与许多（大多数？）罗曼语言一样，欧洲葡萄牙语也有非正式/正式第二人称的划分。巴西葡萄牙语放弃了非正式的第二人称 (tu)，仅使用正式的第二人称 (você)。

现在，因为“thou”已经过时了，所以听起来过于生硬，大多数说英语的人都认为它是正式的第二人称，但实际上它是非正式的形式。所以巴西葡萄牙语和英语都经历了同样的过程，选择了同样的方式。

The formal/informal second person thing is fascinating to me as a Portuguese speaker.

European Portuguese, like many (most?) Romance languages, has the informal/formal second person split. Brazilian Portuguese has dropped the informal second person (tu) and uses only the formal second person (você).

Now, because “thou” is archaic, it sounds overly stiff, and most English speakers assume it was the formal second person, but it was actually the informal form. So both Brazilian Portuguese and English underwent the same process and chose the same way.

在 HN 中查看
Sharlin 2026/04/10 03:45
特别是在英语中，人们将“你”与《英皇钦定本圣经》和类似的基督教文本（“我们的父亲，你在天堂……”）联系在一起，并且可能合理地假设，如果“你”被用来称呼字面上的上帝，那么它一定是正式代词——但使用家庭、非正式的代词正是因为“父亲”的关联！ （OTOH，肯定有一些语言有“tu”/“vous”的区别，孩子们被期望“vous”自己的父母——不知道现在这有多么重要）。

另一个有趣的事情是，称呼你不认识的人为“你”曾经是一种故意的侮辱（“你不值得被称为‘你’”），莎士比亚或其他 EME 文本的现代读者可能会错过这一点。

In English particularly, people associate "thou" with the King James Bible and similar Christian texts ("Our Father, thou art in Heaven…") and might reasonably assume that if "thou" was used to address the literal God, it must have been the formal pronoun – but the familial, informal one was used exactly because of the "father" association! (OTOH there certainly are languages with a tu/vous distinction where children were expected to "vous" their own parents – not sure how much of a thing it is these days).

Another fun thing is that calling someone you don't know "thou" used to be an intentional insult ("you're not worthy of being called 'you'"), something that might be missed by a modern reader of Shakespeare or other EME texts.

aardvark179 2026/04/10 04:52
这在很大程度上取决于您在哪里长大以及您接触过的语言。我的第一个联想是一个非常约克郡的“你知道的”，而不是詹姆斯国王。
It depends a lot on where you were brought up, and the language you were exposed to. My first association would be a very Yorkshire, “Thou knowest,” rather than the king james.

ErroneousBosh 2026/04/10 04:54
>（OTOH，肯定有一些语言有“tu”/“vous”的区别，孩子们被期望“vous”自己的父母——不知道现在这有多么重要）。

有趣的是，在维也纳德语中（我的德语很糟糕，但我至少尝试过），似乎非正式形式是默认的，在一家商店里，我被问到“Braucht du hilfe？”而不是正式的“Kann ich Ihnen helfen？”。

也许这就是他们说维也纳人粗鲁的意思，但来自苏格兰的人即使在相当严肃的环境中使用非正式语言也会显得舒适和正常。

> (OTOH there certainly are languages with a tu/vous distinction where children were expected to "vous" their own parents – not sure how much of a thing it is these days).

It's interesting that in Viennese German (my German is terrible but I do at least try) it seems like the informal form is the default, in a shop I get asked "Braucht du hilfe?" rather than the formal "Kann ich Ihnen helfen?".

Maybe this is what they mean when they say people in Vienna are rude, but coming from Scotland using informal language even in fairly serious settings just seems comfortable and normal.

在 HN 中查看
avadodin 2026/04/10 08:20
这也可能随着时间的推移而改变——就像在人们的记忆中一样。

我奶奶在回忆年轻时去面包店时使用的是正式地址，但现在她会使用熟悉的形式，甚至店员如果对那天有工作感到特别感激的话，最多也会使用假正式的地址。

This can also change with the times —as in, within living memory.

My grandma used the formal address when reminiscing about going to the bakery when she was young but in the present she would use the familiar form and even the clerks would use a fake formal at best if they were feeling particularly grateful for having a job that day.

在 HN 中查看
andrepd 2026/04/10 01:28
而且，“você”原本并不是一个正式的第二人称。从语法上讲，“você”是第三人称单数。它来自“Vossa Mercê”（类似于“Your Mercy”或“Your Grace”），缩写为“vossemeçê”，“você”。其起源是使用第三人称来增加礼貌，并且至今仍然是葡萄牙语中任何正式或半正式语域中常见的语法结构。我想在英语中它也存在，但仅限于最正式的上下文（“这位尊敬的先生同意吗……”）。
Also, "você" is actually not originally a proper formal second person. Grammatically, "você" is a third person singular. It comes from "Vossa Mercê" (something like "Your Mercy" or "Your Grace"), shortened to "vossemeçê", to "você". The origin, and still today a common gramatical construction in Portuguese in any formal or semi-formal register, is to use a periphrase in the third person to increase politeness. I guess in English it also exists, but only on the most fully formal contexts ("Does that right honourable gentleman agree...").
在 HN 中查看
madcaptenor 2026/04/10 01:45
同样，西班牙语“vuestra Merced”演变为“usted”。
Similarly, Spanish "vuestra merced" evolved to "usted".
在 HN 中查看
zoky 2026/04/10 04:34
同样，罗马尼亚语的“dumneavoastră”演变成了……什么都没有，这仍然是罗马尼亚语中“你”的礼貌形式。但有趣的是，它可以用单数和复数形式使用，并且两种形式的动词变位方式完全相同（即第二人称复数）。
And likewise the Romanian “dumneavoastră” evolved into… nothing, that’s still the polite form of “you” in Romanian. Interestingly though, it can be used in both the singular and plural, and takes verbs conjugated exactly the same way for both forms (i.e. the second person plural).
在 HN 中查看
tsimionescu 2026/04/10 11:52
请注意，罗马尼亚语还有第二人称单数正式代词“dumneata”，尽管今天使用它非常罕见，而且实际上不被认为是礼貌的。这可能是因为罗马尼亚语像大多数罗曼语族语言一样，经常在短语中省略主语，因此真正的礼貌标记最终只是使用第二人称复数动词形式来指称单数说话者（“mă puteşi ajuta”更常见，而不是不省略主语的“dumneavoastră mă puteşi ajuta”，而非正式版本是单数“mă poşi ajuta”，其中“dumneata” mă poşi ajuta”也需要 - 所有这些短语的意思是“你可以帮助我”）。

两者的起源更多的是“阁下”（“domnia ta/voastră”）而不是“阁下的仁慈”。

Note that Romanian also has a second person singular formal pronoun, "dumneata", though it's use today is very rare and isn't actually considered polite. This is probably since Romanian, like most Romance languages, often omits the subject in phrases, so the real politeness marker ends up being just the use of second person plural verb forms to refer to a singular speaker ("mă puteți ajuta" is far more common instead of "dumneavoastră mă puteți ajuta" without the omitted subject, while the informal version is the singular "mă poți ajuta", which "dumneata mă poți ajuta" would also require - all of these phrases meaning "can [you] help me").

The origin for both is more "your lordship" ("domnia ta/voastră") than "your mercy", as well.

lemoncucumber 2026/04/10 05:25
早期的贵格会教徒拒绝对不同的人使用不同的第二人称代词，因为这违反了他们的平等主义原则，因此他们称每个人为“你/你”（正如你所料，他们因此陷入了麻烦）。

https://www.nytimes.com/2019/11/16/opinion/sunday/pronouns-q...

Early Quakers rejected using different 2nd person pronouns for different people since it violated their principle of egalitarianism so they called everyone thee/thou (and got into trouble for it as you might expect).

https://www.nytimes.com/2019/11/16/opinion/sunday/pronouns-q...

在 HN 中查看
rmunn 2026/04/10 10:30
有一个关于威廉·佩恩的精彩故事（是的，宾夕法尼亚州的创始人是威廉·佩恩，宾夕法尼亚州也以他的名字命名） 几乎 由于他的贵格会信仰而陷入麻烦，但查理二世国王慷慨地原谅了他。这个故事被写成查尔斯的情妇埃莉诺·“内尔”·格温的传记，可以在这里阅读： https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/The_Story_of_Nell_Gwyn/Chapte...

简而言之，威廉·佩恩拒绝在查尔斯国王面前脱帽，因为他的贵格会信仰是平等主义。如果不是国王摘掉自己的帽子，这会让他因冒犯君主而陷入非常严重的麻烦。 “查尔斯朋友，”佩恩说道（他显然从来没有听说过“当你掉进洞里时就应该停止挖掘”这句话），“你为什么不戴上你的帽子呢？”查理二世国王回答说：“这是这个地方的习俗，一次只能覆盖一个人。”当然，通常情况下是 国王 谁会继续戴上他的王冠。但查尔斯说出这番话后，法庭上没有人能够就这一事件对威廉·佩恩提出冒犯君主的指控。

There's a wonderful story about William Penn (yes, the William Penn who founded Pennsylvania and after whom it was named) nearly getting into trouble for his Quaker beliefs, except that King Charles II graciously forgave him. The story made it into a biography of Charles's mistress Eleanor "Nell" Gwyn, and can be read here: https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/The_Story_of_Nell_Gwyn/Chapte...

Briefly, William Penn refused to take his hat off in the presence of King Charles, due to his Quaker beliefs in egalitarianism. This would have gotten him into very serious trouble for lèse-majesté, except that the king took his own hat off. "Friend Charles," said Penn (who had apparently never heard of the saying that when you're in a hole you should stop digging), "why dost thou not keep on thy hat?" And King Charles II replied, "'Tis the custom of this place that only one person should be covered at a time." Of course, normally it was the king who would keep his crown on. But after Charles said that, nobody in the court could bring a charge of lèse-majesté against William Penn for the incident.

triage8004 2026/04/09 23:09
这很糟糕，因为是的，它是一个错误，或者不是，它不是一个错误，两者都适合
This sucks because yes its a mistake or no its not a mistake both fit
在 HN 中查看
adammarples 2026/04/09 23:55
它们不合适，因为“是”不应该用在“是的，这是一个错误”的上下文中，是的。两个词有助于消除这种歧义。
they don't fit, because 'yes' was not supposed to be used in the context of 'yes it is a mistake', yea was. Having two words helped stop that ambiguity.
在 HN 中查看
card_zero 2026/04/10 00:29
这很令人困惑，因为它被错误地表述了。

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Yes_and_no#The_Early_English_f...

是的 矛盾 否定的问题。那么“这不是一个错误吗？”应该用“是的，这是一个错误”来反驳，或者用“不，这不是一个错误”来肯定。

这更令人困惑，因为我们有以试探性的方式礼貌地提出建议的习语，例如 这不是一个错误吗？ 它已经失去了消极的意义，并开始意味着“我认为这是一个错误”，而不是从字面上解析为“我认为这不是一个错误”。

It's confusing because it was stated wrongly.

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Yes_and_no#The_Early_English_f...

Yes contradicts the negative question. So "Is this not a mistake?" should be contradicted with "yes, it is a mistake" or affirmed with "no, it is not a mistake".

It's further confusing because we have the idiom of suggesting things politely in a tentative manner such as isn't this a mistake? which has lost its sense of negativity and has come to mean "this is a mistake, I think," as opposed to being parsed literally to mean "this is not-a-mistake, I think".

在 HN 中查看
FarmerPotato 2026/04/10 04:00
我正在尝试解析古老的保留语与简单的现代英语中额外的“不”。似乎还带有潜台词。

现代“你现在幸福吗？”用讽刺的语气说，破坏幸福。会很古老地说“你不高兴吗？”仿佛敢于矛盾。它充满了感情，不像同情地说“你不高兴吗？”

其他的：

“你不高兴吗？” “你不是住在哈弗沙姆的那个史密斯吗？” “扎我一下，我不流血吗？”

但通常： “你不是基督徒吗？”很可能看起来很直接，但在正式意义上是“修辞性的”，是一种劝告要像一个人那样行事。

I'm trying to parse the extra "not" in archaic holdovers vs plain modern English. It seems to carry a subtext.

Modern "Are you happy now?" is said with sarcastic tone, to spoil happiness. Would be archaically "Are you not happy?" As if to dare contradiction. It's loaded, unlike when saying sympathetically "Are you unhappy?"

Others:

"Are you not entertained?" "Are you not the very same Smith that dwelt at Haversham?" "Prick me, do I not bleed?"

But commonly: "Are you not a Christian?" most likely seems direct, but said in a formal sense, "rhetorically", an exhortation to act like one.

LAC-Tech 2026/04/10 07:35
值得注意的是，早期的英语中，þu/ģe（thou/ye）并没有表示任何有关形式的意思。它只是单数和复数。
It's worth noting that early on in English, þu/ģe (thou/ye) did not say anything about formality. It was just singular and plural.
在 HN 中查看
matt-attack 2026/04/10 02:07
我们确实见证了代词语料库稀释趋势的又一步。趋势非常明显，从更清晰到更少。

在我的一生中，“他们”和“他们的”都是复数。现在我们已经不再清楚地知道代词是指一个人还是一个以上的人。在看《青春期》时，警察在提到犯罪受害者时提到了“他们”。我错误地认为这一集的大部分时间里都有多个受害者。

显然我适应新定义的速度很慢。

And we’ve literally born witness to yet another step in the trend of diluting our corpus of pronouns. The trend is very clearly from more articulate to less.

“They” and “their” for my whole lifetime were plurals. Now we’ve pretty much lost the mere clarity of knowing if the pronoun means 1 person or more than 1 person. Was watching “Adolescence” and the police mentioned “they” in regards to the victim of a crime. I was mistakenly under the impression that there weee multiple victims for much of the episode.

I’m very clearly slow to adapt to the new definitions.

w10-1 2026/04/10 02:22

我发现自己总是错，我很高兴能得到教训！

The article points out that Chaucer used "they" to refer to singular unknown person, so the usage is very old. It seems more respectful than assuming they are male.

I find myself wrong all the time, and I'm glad for the lesson!

DiogenesKynikos 2026/04/10 08:54
自乔叟以来，“他们”一直被用作单数代词。莎士比亚用过它。狄更斯用过它。

即使那些抱怨单数“他们”的人也会在不注意的时候使用它。这是英语的常规部分。

"They" has been used as a singular pronoun continuously since Chaucer. Shakespeare used it. Dickens used it.

Even people who complain about the singular "they" use it when they're not paying attention. It's a regular part of the English language.

在 HN 中查看
card_zero 2026/04/10 09:06
但没有连续性，在整个时间跨度内并不普遍。

如果这是我们都习惯并感到舒服的东西，为什么还要提到它在遥远的过去就被使用过呢？这篇文章试图同时论证“尝试这个新术语 他们， 它很简单，现在每个人都这么说，它很现代，你会喜欢它”并且“这个术语一点也不奇怪和新鲜，如果你对它感到不舒服，你就很傻，因为它一直在使用。”它试图在争论中两者兼顾。

人们是否也随意使用 它 指的是人类，还是只是我？

But not with continuity, not popularly over that whole time span.

If it's something we're all accustomed to and comfortable with, why even mention that it was being used in the distant past? The article is trying to simultaneously argue "try this new term they, it's easy, everybody's saying it now, it's modern, you'll love it" and "this term is not at all strange and new, you're silly if you feel uncomfortable with it because it has always been used." It's trying to have it both ways in its wrangling.

Do people also casually use it to refer to humans, or is it just me?

etskinner 2026/04/10 03:26

在 HN 中查看
the_biot 2026/04/10 06:01
的确。新鲜的不是将未知性别的人称为“他们”，而是指那些认为自己不具有特定性别并希望被称为“他们”的人。恕我直言，这就是让人感觉很尴尬的部分，不仅仅是他们作为一个人。
Indeed. What's new is not referring to someone of unknown gender as "they", but rather people identifying as non-gender-specific, and wanting to be referred to as "they". That's the part that feels so awkward, IMHO, not simply they as one person.
在 HN 中查看
SoftTalker 2026/04/10 08:43
不，那是不正确的。如果这件外套看起来是男性或女性穿的，请使用他/他或她/她。如果不确定，请使用男性代词，在这种情况下这些代词是性别中性的。
No that's incorrect. Use his/he or her/she if the coat appears to be one that would be worn by a male or female. If uncertain, use male pronouns, which are gender neutral in that scenario.

laichzeit0 2026/04/10 11:21
我肯定在某个地方错过了这个简报，但是有一个非常明显的趋势，即用女性代词“她”取代默认的男性代词来表示性别中立。就在最近，我在 Judea Pearl 的《为什么》一书中注意到了这一点。这种情况何时以及为何开始发生？感觉如此强迫和不自然。你可以感觉到他试图拍某人的屁股或安抚权威。如果你真的很在意的话，至少最好把它混合一下。
I must have missed the brief somewhere, but there was/is a very clear trend to replace the default male pronoun for gender neutrality with the female pronoun she. Just recently I noticed this in Judea Pearl’s Book of Why. When and why did this start happening? It feels so forced and unnatural. You can sense he’s trying to kiss someone’s ass or appease an authority. At least mix it up a bit at best if you truly give a crap.

Incorrect according to who? You? Your Sainted Mother? Sounds innovative to me, and hardly traditional. Saying male is somehow gender neutral sounds even more bizarre.

No, usage makes correctness in language, not people trying to invent some weird conlang they wrongly insist is correct English.

psychoslave 2026/04/09 20:12
我最大的业余项目是关于法语的语法性别，作为一个研究项目发布在维基大学[1]上。

它确实让我经历了很多话题，比如双重、排他/包容的团体人。

仍然在我头脑的一个角落里，有一个想法是引入更多的代词来处理我们所表达的第一人称的更多微妙性[2]。自我不是当前的注意力，也不是与世界其他部分交织在一起的东西，没有它，一切都不存在。

[1] https://fr.wikiversity.org/wiki/Recherche:Sur_l%E2%80%99exte...

[2] 该项目确实提供了一组同质化的扩展代词，比任何小学课本中的两个常规代词多了 6 个。完成所有名词的所有情况是需要完成的最大部分，尽管现在对于最常见的范式已经完成了。

My biggest side project is about grammatical gender in French, published as a research project on wikiversity[1].

It did made me go through many topics, like dual, exclusive/inclusive group person.

Still in a corner of my head, there is the idea to introduce some more pronouns to handle more subtilty about which first person we are expressing about[2]. The ego is not the present attention, nor they are that thing intertwined with the rest of the world without which nothing exists.

[1] https://fr.wikiversity.org/wiki/Recherche:Sur_l%E2%80%99exte...

[2] The project does provide an homogenized extended set of pronouns with 6 more than the two regular ones found in any primary school book. And completing all cases for all nouns is the biggest chunk that need to be completed, though it’s already done by now for the most frequent paradigms.

不知何故，这并不相同。

I found this article quite interesting, and couldn't help but feel there's something that's emotionally lost when we got rid of the dual-forms. The example from Wulf and Eadwacer where "uncer giedd" was translated to "the song of the two of us".

Somehow that just doesn't land the same.

FarmerPotato 2026/04/10 04:09
证书，翻译起来很有挑战性！

《我们的秘密之歌》

“我们共享的歌曲”比较宽松，但上下文有帮助。

“他们正在演奏我们的歌曲”仍然捕捉到了永恒的感觉。但对于这首诗来说是错误的。

Certes, challenging to translate!

"Our secret song"

"Our shared song" is looser, though context helps.

"They're playing our song" still captures the timeless feeling. But is wrong for the poem.

或者“他们两个在一起很好”

frogulis 2026/04/09 19:13
Boy that unc/uncer 看起来非常接近现代德语的 uns/unser。维基词典似乎将其从不同的 PIE 根衍生而来， n̥s vs n̥h——不过我对 PIE 一点也不熟悉。
Boy that unc/uncer looks tantalisingly close to modern German uns/unser. Wiktionary seems to have it descending from a different PIE root, n̥s vs n̥h -- I'm not at all familiar with PIE though.
在 HN 中查看
shakna 2026/04/09 19:44
n̥ 只是“not”前缀。 “ero”是真正的根源。通常，前缀首先适用于词根，然后其他部分才有其含义。 （它是一种重构的语言。既有例外，也有我们不知道的事情。）

“n̥-s-ero-”有点像<“not”next-is-plural“mine”>。

所以，复数-（颠倒我的）。或者大致接近“我们”。

“n̥-h-ero-”有点像<“not”next-is-inclusive-plural“mine”>。

所以，复数-（组（反转我的））。或者大致接近“我们”。

但两者的含义非常接近。 High German 维护了很多 PIE，并且在很多方面都非常接近。不过……威尔士语更接近。

n̥ is just the "not" prefix. The "ero" is the real root. The prefix applies to the root first, and then the other pieces have their meanings, usually. (Its a reconstructed language. There are both exceptions and things we don't know.)

"n̥-s-ero-" is sort of < "not" next-is-plural "mine" >.

So, plural-(invert mine). Or roughly close to "we".

"n̥-h-ero-" is sort of < "not" next-is-inclusive-plural "mine" >.

So, plural-(group (invert mine)). Or roughly close to "us".

But both are pretty close to the same meaning. High German maintained a lot of PIE, and is very close in a lot of ways. Though... Welsh is closer.

shakna 2026/04/10 05:04
讨论日耳曼语言及其继承的两个主要话题可能是：

“从原始印欧语到原始日耳曼语”，林格。

还有更简单的《印欧语助词和代词词干词典》，Dark。

两者都使用“n̥-s-ero-”，尽管采用更传统的 /ˈun.se.rɑz/ 形式。

The two big ones for discussing Germanic languages and their inheritance would probably be:

"From Proto-Indo-European to Proto-Germanic", Ringe.

And the simpler "Lexikon der indogermanischen Partikeln und Pronominalstämme", Dunkel.

Both use "n̥-s-ero-", though in the more traditional /ˈun.se.rɑz/ form.

在 HN 中查看
kmm 2026/04/10 04:28
奇怪的是，古英语 unc 实际上与德语 uns 没有关系，至少在日耳曼语系已经形成之后没有关系。古英语在某个时刻经历了声音变化[1]，其中 -n- 声音在摩擦音之前消失（听起来像 s、f、v、z、sh 等......）。因此，“us”来自较旧的通用形式“uns”，德语继承了该形式，基本没有变化。这种声音变化也解释了英语和德语之间缺少 n 的其他对应关系，例如“mouth-Mund”、“tooth-Zahn”、“other-ander”、“goose-Gans”或“ Five-fünf”。

1： https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ingvaeonic_nasal_spirant_law

Curiously, Old English unc is actually not related to German uns, at least, not after the Germanic language family had already formed. Old English at some point underwent a sound change[1] where the -n- sound disappeared before fricatives (sounds like s, f, v, z, sh, etc...). So "us" comes from an older common form "uns", which German inherited basically unchanged. This sound change also explains other correspondences between English and German where the n is missing, like mouth-Mund, tooth-Zahn, other-ander, goose-Gans or five-fünf.

1: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ingvaeonic_nasal_spirant_law

As a born German, now more native English speaker (left at 8), I agree. But, unless I'm very wrong, uns/unser in modern German is not restricted to 2 people either - it can mean 2 or more, as in "unsere Gemeinde" (our church, referring to something shared by hundreds of people)?

编辑：查看原始日耳曼语人称代词。

tsimionescu 2026/04/10 12:08
根据您链接的页面，它们很明显是假朋友：古英语 unc 与现代德语 uns 无关，它与古日耳曼语 unk 有关（而现代德语 uns 只是古日耳曼语 uns）。
Based on the page you linked, they pretty clearly are false friends: Old English unc is unrelated to modern German uns, it is related instead to Old Germanic unk (while modern German uns is just Old Germanic uns).

trinix912 2026/04/10 01:21
斯洛文尼亚语仍然有语法上的对偶，我们仍然有（并使用）代词，这些代词可以字面翻译为“我们两个”（midva/midve）和“你们两个”（vidva/vidve）等等。有人告诉我，大多数其他斯拉夫语言过去也是如此。
Slovene still has the grammatical dual and we still have (and use) pronouns that could literally be translated as "we two" (midva/midve) and "you two" (vidva/vidve) and so on. I've been told it used to be the same in most other Slavic languages.

tzs 2026/04/10 07:57
如果“wit”指的是两个人，我想知道“halfwit”是否有关联。事实证明并非如此。 “Wit”这个代词和“wit”这个指智力能力的名词是不相关的同音异义词，“halfwit”来自后者。
If "wit" meant two people, I wondered if halfwit could be related. Turns out it isn't. "Wit" the pronoun and "wit" the noun referring to mental ability are unrelated homonyms, and halfwit comes from the latter.

madcaptenor 2026/04/09 22:17
在我搬到南方之前，我（一个非南方人）不太愿意说“你们”。 但“你们这些家伙”似乎有性别歧视。 我在南方待了十年，并没有学会太多的方言，但我现在肯定会说“你们大家”。

如果有“W'all”就太好了。 我想这不是一个东西，因为它听起来太像分隔房间的东西了。

Before I moved to the South I (a non-Southerner) did not feel comfortable saying "y'all". But "you guys" seemed sexist. I have since spent a decade in the South and I have not picked up much of the dialect, but I definitely say "y'all" now.

"W'all" would be nice to have. I guess it's not a thing because it sounds too much like the things that separate rooms.

在 HN 中查看
lamasery 2026/04/09 23:48
“Guys”（前面没有“the”）在大多数情况下（至少在美国的某些地区）毫无争议地是中性的。我不确定担心它的人们是否来自绝对不使用它的地方，或者根本不使用它的地方，所以他们不知道在（至少很多）有它的地方这不是问题。

不过我更喜欢“你们大家”。我认为这是我们拥有的最好的选择（“你”是另一个大选择，呃，恶心）

我也喜欢“你们”和“你们大家”的细微差别。

"Guys" (without a "the" in front of it) is uncontroversially gender-neutral in most contexts in at least some parts of the US. I'm not sure whether folks worried about it are from places where it's definitely not, or places where it's not used much at all so they're not aware that it's a non-issue in (at least many) places where it is.

I do prefer "y'all", though. I think it's the best one we've got, of the options ("yous" being another big one, and ew, gross)

I also love the nuance of "y'all" and "all y'all".

madcaptenor 2026/04/09 22:53
不。据我所知，单数“y'all”，当它存在时，是隐含的复数。 例如，当您走进一家餐馆并说“你们都有可乐吗？”时，您可能会听到单数“你们”。服务员——这不仅仅指服务员，还指整个餐厅。 但我不是语言学家，而且我也没有花太多时间和南方话比较重的人打交道，所以你不应该相信我说的话。
No. As far as I can tell, singular "y'all", when it exists, is an implied plural. What you might hear as singular "y'all" is, say, when you go into a restaurant and say "do y'all have Coke?" to the server - that doesn't refer to just the server but to the restaurant as a whole. But I'm not a linguist and also I don't spend much time among people with heavier Southern dialect, so you shouldn't believe what I say.

saltcured 2026/04/10 01:26
有人向我解释这是南方人之间的东西方分歧。当您穿过德克萨斯州时，越来越多的人认为您需要“all y'all”来表示复数。

这是那些西部南方人告诉我的。我不知道语言学家是否会同意，但这似乎是一些实际语言使用者的理解......

我所知道的是，在德克萨斯州、新墨西哥州和亚利桑那州的某个地方还有第二个边界，因为我从未见过一个土生土长的加州人会非讽刺地说“你们”。

I've had it explained to me as a western/eastern divide among southerners. As you head through Texas, more people think you need "all y'all" for plurals.

That's something those western southerners told me. I don't know if a linguist would agree, but that seems to be the understanding of some actual language users...

All I know is that there is a second boundary somewhere through TX, NM, and AZ, because I've never met a native Californian who would say "y'all" non ironically.

pessimizer 2026/04/09 23:48
不，你说得对。很多人试图表现得可爱，让南方语言看起来比实际更陌生，这是过度“纠正”。

当南方人对一个人说 y’all 时，他们实际上是在对你说话 和你的家人 （即使你可能是那里唯一的一个人。）如果我问“你们好吗？”我想知道你和你的家人过得怎么样。

No, you've got it right. A lot of people trying to be cute and make southern language seem more alien than it is are over-"correcting."

When southern people say y'all to one person, they're really addressing you and your family (even though you might be the only one there.) If I ask "how y'all doing?" I want to know how you and yours are doing.

You, y'all (small close group), y'all all (larger, further group), and "all y'all" — Southeast Texas (coastal) dialect form that showed up about 25 yrs ago. I suspect it might've been there all along, but only became acceptable at that point?

Another 100+ years, and this'll be some solid grammar.

在 HN 中查看
gibspaulding 2026/04/09 21:32
别忘了你或因兹！

当我还是一名学校老师时，我曾为此苦苦挣扎。英语缺乏一种很好的方式来澄清你是在向一群人还是一个人讲话，这在课堂上经常出现。 “Class, you…”很笨拙，“You people…”有明显的问题，y'all 或任何其他缩写通常被认为是糟糕的语法。 我一般都是和你们一起去的。孩子们会对此大笑，但这似乎有助于引起他们的注意。

Don’t forget you’uns or yinz!

I struggled with this when I was a school teacher. English lacks a good way to clarify you are addressing a group vs one person, which comes up a lot in a classroom. “Class, you…” is clunky, “You guys…” has obvious issues, and y’all or any other contraction is generally considered bad grammar. I generally went with y’all. Kids would laugh about it, but that seemed to help get their attention.

在 HN 中查看
dfxm12 2026/04/09 21:56
当然，您知道所有学生的名字，如果您对一个人讲话，您可以使用他们的名字。将类仅仅称为“类”似乎也足够了。我很难想象你被迫含糊地使用“你”的情况。
Surely, you knew all of your students' names and if you were addressing one person, you could've used their name. Addressing the class as merely "class" seems adequate as well. I'm having a hard time thinking of a situation where you are forced to use "you" ambiguously.

madcaptenor 2026/04/09 22:54

No, because "you in the back" could refer to just one person in the back, instead of several. So "y'all in the back" is more specific. (Of course names are an option in this context.)
在 HN 中查看
dfxm12 2026/04/09 23:09
（当然，在这种情况下，名称是一个选项。）

是的，在这种情况下，您不必在这种情况下模糊地使用“您”。

不，因为“后面的你”可能只指后面的一个人，而不是几个。

如果你想对一个人说话，你就会说出那个人的名字，而不是主动地给情况带来歧义。上下文和肢体语言也使这一点显而易见。如果你指的是一个人，你会与一个人而不是一群人进行眼神交流，等等。学生也知道他们是否在集中注意力。 “背部”并不是一个特定的区域。

(Of course names are an option in this context.)

Yes, this is a case where you aren't forced to use "you" ambiguously in that context.

No, because "you in the back" could refer to just one person in the back, instead of several.

If you meant to address one person, you'd have said that one person's name, instead of voluntarily introducing ambiguity to the situation. Context & body language also makes this obvious. If you meant one person, you'd be making eye contact with one person instead of a group of people, etc. Students also know if they're paying attention or not. "The back" is not a specific area.

iterateoften 2026/04/09 22:38
有趣的是，在英语中，我们有专门的代词来表示恰好为 2 的复数，但在俄语中，对于小于 5 的复数，它们有特殊的变格。

这很重要吗？我不知道。是否存在一种语言，其特殊情况恰好为 2，另一种情况为“少数”，另一种情况为“很多”？ 比较不同文化很有趣。

Interesting that in English we had special pronoun for plurals of exactly 2, but in Russian for instance they have special case declensions for plurals less than 5.

Is that significant? I have no idea. Is there a language with special case for exactly 2 with another case for a “few” and with yet another for “a lot”? Interesting to compare different cultures.

在 HN 中查看
kmm 2026/04/10 04:43
俄语曾经也有双代词，但它们都在 13 世纪的某个地方消失了，就像斯洛文尼亚语以外的所有其他斯拉夫语言一样。

用于小数字的系统可能是早期名词双数的广泛扩展，即类似于复数但仅用于两件事。对于（某些）男性名词，主格双重词尾与属格单数相同，然后扩展到所有其他名词，即使这种对应关系不成立，也从仅 2 件事扩展到 3 和 4。如今，名词的双数形式已被完全遗忘，该规则的唯一解释是它是单数属格。

Russian used to have dual pronouns too, but they all were lost somewhere in the 13th century, as in all other Slavic languages other than Slovenian.

The system used for small numbers is probably a broad extension of an earlier dual number for nouns, i.e. something like a plural but just for two things. For (some) male nouns, the nominative dual ending was the same as the genitive singular, which was then extended to all other nouns even when this correspondence didn't hold, and from just 2 things to 3 and 4 as well. Nowadays the dual has been completely forgotten for nouns, and the only interpretation of the rule is that it's a genitive singular.

在 HN 中查看
stevula 2026/04/10 00:11
现代英语仅通过单数/复数来区分语法数字（古英语有双数），而某些语言甚至有审判（三）。

俄语区分 paucal（少数）和复数（许多）。这不是很常见，但还有一些其他语言可以做到这一点。

Whereas modern English only distinguishes grammatical number by singular/plural (and Old English had dual), some languages even have trial (three).

Russian distinguishes paucal (few) from plural (many). It’s not super common but there are some other languages that do it.

在 HN 中查看
andrewshadura 2026/04/10 00:02
它不仅仅是5，还包括21到25、31到35等。但是，一些斯拉夫语言（例如斯洛伐克语和捷克语）不这样做，只有5以下的特殊数字。
It’s not just 5, it’s also 21 to 25, 31 to 35 etc. However, some Slavic languages (e.g. Slovak and Czech) don’t do that, and only have those special numerals for under 5.

stoneman24 2026/04/09 19:32
我想知道它是如何演变成现代英国俚语“git”的。引用维基百科[0]

“现代英国英语俚语，git (/ɡɪt/) 是一个侮辱性术语，用于描述某人（通常是男人）被认为是愚蠢、无能、烦人、不愉快或愚蠢的人。”。

并且 “ Git 是 Linus Torvalds 创建的一款流行的开源版本控制软件。Torvalds 开玩笑地将其命名为“git”，后来又将其定义为“愚蠢的内容跟踪器”。

[0] https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Git_(slang)

I wonder how it evolved into the modern British slang of “git”. To quote Wikipedia [0]

“modern British English slang, a git (/ɡɪt/) is a term of insult used to describe someone—usually a man—who is considered stupid, incompetent, annoying, unpleasant, or silly.“.

And “ Git is a popular open-source software for version control created by Linus Torvalds. Torvalds jokingly named it "git" after the slang term, later defining it as "the stupid content tracker".”

[0] https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Git_(slang)

dataflow 2026/04/09 22:12
阿拉伯语有双主语代词。我想知道这个概念是独立发展的还是有某种影响？
Arabic has dual subject pronouns. I wonder if the concept developed independently or if there was any influence somehow?
在 HN 中查看
malikolivier 2026/04/10 06:48
需要注意的是，只有现代标准阿拉伯语（以古兰经使用的语言为基础的现代通用阿拉伯语）仍然具有双重性。 从这种形式的阿拉伯语演变而来的大多数（如果不是全部？）口语方言已经失去了双重性。

有趣的是，我们注意到许多语言中都有类似的丢失对偶的模式。找到相反的模式将是非常有趣的：一种新出现的对偶语言。然而，我不知道这样的语言。

It should be noted that only Modern Standard Arabic (the modern common Arabic language based on the language used in the Qur'an) still has dual. Most (if not all?) spoken dialects, which evolved from this form of Arabic have already lost dual.

It's interesting that we notice a similar pattern of losing the dual in many languages. And it would be very interesting to find the opposite pattern: a language where the dual newly develops out of nowhere. However, I do not know of such language.

在 HN 中查看
Two9A 2026/04/09 22:26
阿拉伯语属于假设的原始印欧语言的闪语分支，具有双数： https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dual_(grammatical_number)

因此，你会期望看到从西欧到南亚的语言要么具有双重概念，要么有经过证实的祖先。

Arabic is on the Semitic branch of the hypothesised proto-Indo-European language, which has dual number: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dual_(grammatical_number)

So you'd expect to see languages from western Europe to south Asia that either have the dual concept, or have an attested ancestor that did.

在 HN 中查看
another-dave 2026/04/09 22:59
在印欧语系中，爱尔兰语有对偶的概念。它与成对出现的事物一起使用，例如“mo dhá láimh”——我的两只手。

有趣的是，要说单手，你会说“leath-lámh”，其中_leath_表示一半，所以一半<通常是一对的东西>。

Within Indo-European languages, Irish has the concept of the dual. It's used with things that come in pairs like "mo dhá láimh" - my two hands.

Interestingly, to say one-handed you'd say "leath-lámh", where _leath_ means half, so half the <thing that's usually one of a pair>.
